nuscale_ch01_0791.title: SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1: Radiological Consequences of Fuel Handling AccidentsII.2 Radioactivity Control Features of Fuel Storage and Handling Systems
nuscale_ch01_0791.summary: This section evaluates the NuScale design’s conformance with regulatory criteria regarding radiological consequences of fuel handling accidents, specifically focusing on radioactivity control features of fuel storage and handling systems. It concludes that only the criteria related to systems inside the Reactor Building are applicable, as NuScale’s design does not use a traditional containment building for fuel storage and handling; therefore, some acceptance criteria are not applicable or only partially conform.

nuscale_ch01_0034.title: Reactor Building
nuscale_ch01_0034.summary: The Reactor Building (RXB) is centrally located on the NuScale Power Plant site and houses the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) along with essential systems for plant operation and shutdown. It is a large, reinforced concrete, Seismic Category I structure designed for safety against various hazards, and provides radiation protection for personnel. Each NPM is placed in its own bay within a common reactor pool, arranged in two rows along the pool’s north and south walls.

nuscale_ch01_0036.title: Refueling Operations
nuscale_ch01_0036.summary: Refueling operations for a NuScale Power Module (NPM) are conducted independently from other modules. The process involves moving the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling pool, disassembling it for inspection, maintenance, and refueling, and then reassembling and returning it to service after successful testing and startup procedures.

nuscale_ch01_0031.title: Plant Cooling Water Systems
nuscale_ch01_0031.summary: The plant cooling water systems in the NuScale design include several nonsafety-related systems that support plant operations by transferring heat from various components to the site cooling water system. These systems consist of the reactor component cooling water system (RCCWS), reactor and spent fuel pool cooling systems, and an open-loop circulating water system that supplies cooling water to the turbine condensers and removes excess heat via mechanical-draft cooling towers.

nuscale_ch01_0309.title: SRP 9.1.4, Rev 4: Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity DesignII.3 Specific Criteria to Meet GDC 61
nuscale_ch01_0309.summary: This section documents NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity Design, specifically addressing compliance with General Design Criterion (GDC) 61. It confirms that the design meets the required safety standards outlined in SRP 9.1.4, Revision 4, with no exceptions or additional comments noted.

nuscale_ch01_0792.title: SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1: Radiological Consequences of Fuel Handling AccidentsII.3 Dose Model and Modeling Assumptions
nuscale_ch01_0792.summary: This section explains that the acceptance criteria for the dose model and modeling assumptions in SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1 are not applicable to the NuScale design because they have been superseded by the updated criteria in SRP Section 15.0.3. As a result, the specific radiological analysis methodology and assumptions outlined in SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1 do not apply to the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report.

nuscale_ch01_0037.title: Control Building
nuscale_ch01_0037.summary: The section describes the refueling operations for a NuScale Power Module (NPM), which are conducted independently for each module. The process involves moving the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling area, disassembling it for inspection, maintenance, and refueling, and then reassembling and returning it to service after successful testing and inspection.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and nominal parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It highlights differences in aspects such as electrical output, core size, fuel assemblies, reactor coolant system, and safety systems, using summary tables for direct comparison. The data demonstrates that NuScale's design is significantly smaller, simpler, and incorporates different safety and operational features compared to conventional PWRs.

nuscale_ch01_0410.title: SRP 9.5.4, Rev 3: Emergency Diesel Engine Fuel Oil Storage and Transfer SystemAll (II.1 thru II.4) Compliance with GDC 2, GDC 4, GDC 5, and GDC 17
nuscale_ch01_0410.summary: The NuScale plant design does not include or require safety-related emergency diesel generators, and therefore the associated systems (fuel oil storage and transfer, cooling water, starting, lubrication, and combustion air intake/exhaust) and their compliance with the specified General Design Criteria (GDC) are not applicable. The plant’s safety functions do not rely on either AC or DC power sources.

nuscale_ch01_0298.title: DSRS 9.1.2, Rev 0: New and Spent Fuel StorageII.7 Criticality Monitors and Subcriticality Margin
nuscale_ch01_0298.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for new and spent fuel storage, specifically addressing criticality monitors and subcriticality margin. It details compliance with various General Design Criteria (GDC) and regulatory requirements, noting that the design meets or partially meets the specified criteria, with references to relevant sections for further information. The section also clarifies that certain requirements are not applicable due to the passive nature of the plant design.

nuscale_ch01_0799.title: SRP 15.7.5, Rev 2: Spent Fuel Cask Drop AccidentsII.4 ESF Grade Atmosphere Clean-Up System in Spent Fuel Storage Area
nuscale_ch01_0799.summary: The section explains that the RBC system in the spent fuel storage area is designed to be single-failure-proof according to NUREG-0612 guidelines, ensuring that no credible single component failure can result in dropping a critical load. Because of this robust crane design, there is no need to evaluate or analyze the radiological consequences of a spent fuel cask drop accident, and thus no accident analysis has been performed for such scenarios.

edges.description: Section A outlines regulatory criteria and conformance for radiological control during fuel handling accidents, while Section B describes the physical layout and systems (including waste management) in the reactor building that implement these controls, connecting regulatory requirements to facility design.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and radiological safety requirements for fuel handling accidents, while Section B describes the specific refueling operations process that must comply with those criteria.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria and radiological controls for fuel handling accidents, while Section B describes the plant cooling water systems that are operationally relevant during fuel handling and refueling outages, connecting them through the operational context of fuel handling processes.

Section A addresses the radiological consequences and regulatory conformance of fuel handling accidents, while Section B specifies the design criteria for the systems (light load handling and refueling cavity) that enable safe fuel handling, thus Section B provides the technical prerequisites and requirements that underpin the accident analyses discussed in Section A.

Section A describes the radioactivity control features of fuel storage and handling systems, while Section B outlines the dose model and modeling assumptions used to evaluate radiological consequences during fuel handling accidents, with the control features in A directly informing the assumptions and parameters used in B's dose modeling.

Section A provides the general layout and arrangement of the reactor building, which establishes the physical context necessary for understanding the specific refueling operations described in Section B.

Section A describes the general arrangement of major plant structures, which provides context for where fuel handling occurs, while Section B outlines the plant cooling water systems, which are relevant to supporting safe fuel handling operations during outages.

Section A provides a general description of the plant layout and references where detailed systems (including those relevant to fuel handling) are discussed, while Section B specifies the regulatory criteria and conformance requirements for systems directly involved in the fuel handling process.

Section A provides the general plant layout and references where radioactive waste management and effluent monitoring systems are detailed, while Section B outlines the regulatory criteria and modeling assumptions for evaluating radiological consequences of fuel handling accidents, connecting them through the need to understand plant systems and arrangements (from A) to assess compliance with accident analysis requirements (in B).

Section A describes the specific steps of the refueling process for an individual NPM, while Section B notes that NPMs can be taken offline individually for refueling, thus Section B provides general context that supports the operational independence described in Section A.

Section A describes the specific refueling operations process for the NuScale plant, while Section B lists the regulatory criteria (SRP 9.1.4) that the refueling and handling systems must meet, connecting the operational steps in A to the compliance requirements in B.

Section A describes the specific steps of the refueling (fuel handling) process, while Section B outlines the regulatory criteria and dose modeling assumptions that must be considered to evaluate the radiological consequences of such fuel handling operations.

Section A mentions that NPMs can be taken offline individually for refueling outages, which is a prerequisite operational context for the fuel handling process described in Section B's regulatory criteria for refueling cavity and handling system design.

Section A describes the operational flexibility of NPMs, including their ability to be taken offline for refueling outages, which is a prerequisite for the fuel handling process analyzed in Section B's regulatory criteria for fuel handling accident consequences.

Section A outlines the design and regulatory criteria for the fuel handling and refueling systems, while Section B evaluates the radiological consequences of fuel handling accidents, thus Section A's design features are intended to satisfy the safety requirements assessed in Section B.

Section A describes the specific steps and equipment involved in the fuel handling (refueling) process, while Section B outlines the regulatory criteria and safety analysis requirements (such as radiological consequences) that the fuel handling process must satisfy.

Section A describes the refueling operations and movement of the NPM using the RBC, while Section B provides the general arrangement of major structures and equipment, including the layout relevant to the fuel handling process described in Section A.

Section A provides design features and parameters of the NuScale plant, which are relevant for evaluating the radiological control features and regulatory conformance discussed in Section B regarding fuel handling accidents.

Section A provides nominal design parameters for the NuScale plant and compares them to typical PWRs, while Section B describes the general arrangement and systems within the reactor building, with both sections offering contextual information relevant to understanding the fuel handling process but without directly referencing or specifying each other.

Section A provides general design features and parameters of the NuScale plant, which set the context for the specific refueling operations described in Section B by establishing the plant's structural and operational framework.

Section A discusses the inapplicability of emergency diesel engine fuel oil storage and transfer systems to the NuScale design, while Section B addresses the radiological consequences and control features of fuel storage and handling systems, thus both sections are related through their focus on fuel handling systems but address different aspects—Section A on system requirements and Section B on safety analysis.

Section A describes the specific refueling operations process for the NuScale plant, while Section B provides a comparison of NuScale's design features and parameters (including potentially the refueling process) with those of a typical PWR, connecting them through the operational details and design values relevant to fuel handling.

Section A provides a general comparison of NuScale plant design features with typical PWRs, while Section B specifies that certain emergency diesel generator systems and their regulatory requirements are not applicable to the NuScale design due to its unique features, thus Section B provides specific regulatory context for the design differences highlighted in Section A.

Section A addresses the design and operational requirements for fuel storage and handling systems, including criticality and power supply considerations, while Section B evaluates the radiological consequences and control features associated with fuel handling accidents, thus Section A's design provisions directly support the safety objectives assessed in Section B.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and safety analysis requirements for fuel storage and handling, while Section B provides a general description of the plant layout and systems, including where fuel handling and storage occur, thus Section B specifies the physical context for the regulatory requirements described in Section A.

Section B describes the specific steps and equipment involved in the NuScale fuel handling and refueling process, while Section A provides regulatory criteria and safety requirements (such as criticality monitoring and power source considerations) that must be satisfied during these operations.

Section A discusses the design features (single-failure-proof crane) that eliminate the need for accident analysis during spent fuel cask handling, while Section B addresses the radiological control features and requirements for fuel handling accidents, connecting them through their focus on safety measures and regulatory conformance during fuel handling operations.

Section A describes the design features and safety criteria for handling spent fuel casks, specifically referencing the single-failure-proof crane that eliminates the need for accident analysis, while Section B provides a general description of the plant layout and the systems, including radioactive waste management, that support and interface with fuel handling operations.

Section B describes the operational use of the RBC crane during refueling, while Section A specifies that the RBC crane is designed to be single-failure-proof, ensuring safe fuel handling by preventing load drop accidents during such operations.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and design considerations for fuel storage and handling systems, while Section B describes how the design (specifically the single-failure-proof crane) satisfies these criteria by eliminating the need for certain accident analyses, thus connecting the regulatory requirements to their implementation in the fuel handling process.
